evals.prompt: I'm building a B2B SaaS tool for restaurant inventory management. We charge $299/month per location. There are roughly 660,000 full-service restaurant locations in the US. Based on data from the National Restaurant Association, about 12% use dedicated inventory software today, and realistically I can capture 1-2% of the addressable market in the next 2 years. Can you do a full TAM SAM SOM analysis using bottom-up methodology?
evals.expected_output: A Market Sizing Summary table with concrete dollar values for TAM, SAM, and SOM (TAM > SAM > SOM), explicitly named Bottom-Up methodology, step-by-step calculations showing the unit economics (660,000 locations × $299/month × 12 months), Key Assumptions section with at least 2 numbered assumptions, a Data Sources section naming at least one source, and a Confidence Level section.

deterministic_checks.description: Methodology is explicitly named as Bottom-Up
Output contains a Market Sizing Summary section header
At least one concrete dollar value is present (no $X or $XXM placeholders)
User-provided price of $299/month appears verbatim in calculations

expectations: TAM value is numerically larger than SAM, which is numerically larger than SOM — the ordering hierarchy is correct
The bottom-up calculation shows the multiplication chain: locations × price × 12 months to arrive at revenue figures
The 660,000 restaurant location figure from the prompt appears in the calculation
The 12% penetration assumption is used to derive SAM from TAM (or an equivalent narrowing factor is explained)
Key Assumptions section contains at least 2 numbered assumptions specific to this scenario, not generic filler
National Restaurant Association (or another named source) is cited in the Data Sources section

evals.prompt: What's the TAM SAM SOM for the global cybersecurity training software market? I'm targeting mid-market companies (100-2,500 employees) in North America. I've seen Gartner estimates that put the overall security awareness training market at around $5.6B globally in 2024 with a 15% CAGR projected through 2029. Use a top-down approach and give me bear/base/bull scenarios.
evals.expected_output: Top-down market sizing with Gartner's $5.6B figure as the TAM anchor, SAM narrowed to North America mid-market, SOM as a realistic near-term achievable share. Bear/Base/Bull scenario table with Bear < Base < Bull for all metrics. Growth table showing CAGR with INC trend indicator. No placeholder values.

deterministic_checks.description: Methodology is explicitly named as Top-Down
INC trend indicator is present (growing market)
User-provided Gartner $5.6B figure appears in output
Market Sizing Summary section header is present

expectations: TAM is grounded at or near the $5.6B Gartner figure (or projected forward at 15% CAGR if a future year is chosen), not an invented number
SAM is meaningfully smaller than TAM, reflecting geographic restriction to North America and segment restriction to mid-market (100-2,500 employees)
Bear scenario TAM/SAM/SOM values are each strictly less than the corresponding Base scenario values, which are strictly less than Bull scenario values
The scenario table does not use placeholder values — all three scenarios have concrete dollar figures
Gartner is named as a data source in the Data Sources section
The 15% CAGR appears with supporting evidence or the Gartner citation, not as an unsupported claim

evals.prompt: We're building software to prevent data breaches for healthcare organizations. According to IBM's 2024 Cost of a Data Breach Report, the average healthcare breach costs $9.8M. Our product can reduce breach probability by about 60%. We'd capture maybe 15% of prevented breach value as our price. There are approximately 6,000 hospitals in the US and maybe 100,000 smaller healthcare providers. Can you size this market using value theory / willingness-to-pay methodology?
evals.expected_output: Value Theory sizing with the $9.8M breach cost figure used as the pain-point anchor. Calculation chain: breach cost × reduction % × capture rate × customer count. Full TAM/SAM/SOM table with concrete dollar values. IBM cited as a data source. Explicit capture rate of 15% stated and used. No placeholders.

deterministic_checks.description: Methodology is named as Value Theory or willingness-to-pay
User-provided $9.8M breach cost figure appears in output
The 15% capture rate appears in the calculation
Market Sizing Summary section header is present
IBM is cited as a data source

expectations: The value chain calculation is shown explicitly: breach cost ($9.8M) × risk reduction (60%) × capture rate (15%) forms the per-customer revenue basis
TAM is numerically larger than SAM, which is numerically larger than SOM
The customer universe (6,000 hospitals + 100,000 smaller providers) is referenced and used to bound the sizing
The capture rate of 15% is explicitly labeled and used — not silently absorbed into the math
IBM's 2024 Cost of a Data Breach Report is named in the Data Sources section
The Confidence Level section explains why value theory introduces uncertainty (e.g., breach probability estimates are variable, not all healthcare orgs face equal risk)

evals.prompt: I need market sizing for the physical DVD rental kiosk business in the US. I know this is a declining market — Redbox basically collapsed. Give me a realistic TAM SAM SOM analysis with the decline rate and any scenario range you think is appropriate. I'm looking at a niche operator play targeting rural areas with no streaming access.
evals.expected_output: Declining market analysis using Top-Down with DEC trend indicator and a negative CAGR. Full TAM/SAM/SOM with concrete dollar values. DEC trend documented with supporting data. Scenario range (at minimum bear/base). Entry/operating risks noted. No placeholders. TAM > SAM > SOM maintained despite decline.

deterministic_checks.description: DEC trend indicator is present for the declining market
A negative growth rate or declining CAGR is explicitly stated
Market Sizing Summary section header is present
Entry or operating risks are mentioned
No placeholder values despite the declining/uncertain market

expectations: TAM is numerically larger than SAM, which is numerically larger than SOM — the hierarchy is maintained even in a declining market
The DEC trend indicator is paired with a concrete negative rate (e.g., -12% CAGR or -15% YoY) and a supporting data point such as Redbox's collapse or industry contraction
The rural niche framing from the prompt is reflected in the SAM derivation — SAM should be meaningfully smaller than TAM, scoped to low-streaming-penetration geographies
At least one scenario (bear or full bear/base) is provided with concrete values, not a single point estimate presented as certain
Entry and operating risks specific to a declining market (supply chain, consumer behavior, streaming displacement) are named explicitly
Confidence Level is Low or Medium, with a clear explanation tied to the market's contraction and limited available data
